query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Venice faces significant threats from Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR), which combines climatic sea level rise with local land subsidence. Research indicates that the frequency of flooding and the operational sustainability of the MOSE barrier system are heavily dependent on IPCC emission scenarios and the total rise reached by the end of the century. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/)

### 1. Sea Level Rise Projections
Projections for Venice vary by scenario, with median relative sea level (RSL) increases expected as follows:
*   **By 2050:** RSL is projected to reach approximately 15 cm (RCP 2.6) to 17 cm (RCP 7.0). [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)
*   **By 2100 (Low Emission - RCP 2.6):** Likely range of 32 cm to 62 cm, with a median of approximately 42–47 cm. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/) [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)
*   **By 2100 (High Emission - RCP 8.5/SSP5-8.5):** Likely range of 58 cm to 110 cm, with a median of ~81 cm. A high-end scenario involving ice-sheet melting could see RSL rise exceeding 180 cm. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/)
*   **By 2150:** Under SSP5-8.5, extreme water levels—including tides and storm surges—could temporarily reach 3.47 m, potentially submerging up to 65% of the lagoon. [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - Abstract](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract)

Local subsidence contributes significantly to these figures, adding approximately 10 cm by 2100, though rates at the MOSE barrier inlets are higher (up to 2.9 mm/yr) due to construction-related factors. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/) [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - Abstract](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract)

### 2. Flood Frequency Projections
Historically, Venice has seen a surge in exceptional floods (>140 cm). Future flood frequency is tied to MOSE activation, which is currently triggered at a threshold of 110 cm. [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)
*   **Baseline (2020):** Approximately 6 closures per year. [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)
*   **2050:** Closures are expected to rise to between 24 (RCP 2.6) and 28 (RCP 7.0) per year. [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)
*   **2100:** Projections indicate a dramatic increase to 209 closures annually under RCP 2.6 and up to 353 closures under RCP 7.0. [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1) Other researchers suggest that over 200 annual closures could total approximately 1,000 hours of isolation from the sea. [The Impact of MOSE Flood Barriers on Microphytobenthic Community of the Venice Lagoon](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10143115/)

### 3. MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Operational Limits
The MOSE system protects Venice from surges up to 3 meters, but its long-term viability is threatened by the frequency of closures required as sea levels rise. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/)
*   **Operational Thresholds:** While the general threshold is 110 cm, some research indicates that partial closures of only the Lido inlet can manage smaller events up to +0.4 m RSLR, though this becomes ineffective during strong south-easterly winds. [Exploring the partial use of the Mo.S.E. system as effective adaptation to rising flood frequency of Venice](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/3629/)
*   **The 50-60 cm Critical Threshold:** If RSLR reaches 50–60 cm, the barriers would need to close so frequently (~300 times/year) that the lagoon would essentially become a permanent flood wall. This would halt port activities and cause severe ecological damage, including oxygen depletion and toxic sediment shifts. [Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/) [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1) [The Impact of MOSE Flood Barriers on Microphytobenthic Community of the Venice Lagoon](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10143115/)
*   **Economic Cost:** Each MOSE closure is estimated to cost nearly €1 million (including operating costs and economic damage to port activities). [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)

### 4. Alternative Adaptation
Given the operational limits of MOSE, studies have evaluated lifting the city of Venice by 30 cm through seawater injection. This adaptation could reduce the number of annual closures from 308 to 44 under RCP 4.5 by 2100, potentially offering a more sustainable long-term solution than relying solely on the barriers. [The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_1dad3b3d-f534-44ed-96e4-4a9861977314?preview=1)

sources.title: Sea-level rise and flood prediction in Venice: Trends and projections (2021 Review)
sources.content:
The projected impacts of sea-level rise (SLR) on Venice, flood frequency, and MOSE barrier effectiveness are synthesized from two key 2021 review articles (Zanchettin et al.; Umgiesser et al.):

### 1. Sea Level Projections for 2100 (Relative to 1986–2005)
Projections for Venetian relative sea level (RSL) by 2100 vary by IPCC emission scenario and include both climatically induced rise and local subsidence:
*   **RCP 2.6 (Low Emission):** Likely range of 32 cm to 62 cm (median ~47 cm).
*   **RCP 8.5 (High Emission):** Likely range of 58 cm to 110 cm (median ~81 cm).
*   **High-End Scenario:** A plausible but unlikely scenario involving strong ice-sheet melting could result in an RSL rise exceeding 180 cm (up to 2 meters with adverse regional atmospheric forcing).
*   **Subsidence Contribution:** Natural local subsidence is projected to add approximately 10 cm to these totals by 2100. Historical data (1872–2019) shows an average RSL rise of 2.5 mm/year, with subsidence contributing about half of this historical trend.

### 2. Flood Frequency and Extreme Events
*   **Historical Context:** Exceptional flooding events (water levels >140 cm) have occurred 25 times in the last 150 years, but their frequency has surged recently. 2019 saw the second-highest level ever measured at 189 cm (the record is 194 cm in 1966).
*   **Future Trends:** SLR will dramatically increase the frequency of flooding. Without barriers, even spring tides could cause flooding in St. Mark's Square (already flooded at +80 cm) without any meteorological contribution.

### 3. MOSE Barrier effectiveness
The MOSE (MOdulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) system consists of 78 mobile gates at the three lagoon inlets designed to protect Venice from surges up to 3 meters.
*   **Current Status:** MOSE began preliminary operations in 2020 and was fully functional by late 2021. In its first three uses (October 2020), it successfully protected the city from flooding during storm surges.
*   **Operational Constraints:** Decisions to close are made 4–6 hours before a peak, typically at a threshold of 110 cm. This creates a conflict between protecting the city and allowing maritime traffic.
*   **Long-term Effectiveness under SLR:**
    *   **At 50 cm SLR:** The number of closures would increase to approximately 300 per year. This level of operation would effectively close the lagoon to tidal flushing for much of the year, posing severe ecological risks (e.g., lack of oxygenation) and halting port activities.
    *   **At 100 cm SLR:** Under high-end RCP 8.5 scenarios, the lagoon would need to be closed nearly permanently, rendering the current mobile barrier strategy unsustainable for maintaining a healthy lagoon ecosystem.
    *   **Subsidence Risk:** MOSE construction itself has caused local subsidence around the inlets at rates up to 70 mm/year, which may affect the long-term structural alignment and maintenance of the barriers.

sources.title: Exploring the partial use of the Mo.S.E. system as effective adaptation to rising flood frequency of Venice
sources.content:
### Sea Level Rise and Flood Frequency Projections
- **Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR):** Venice is vulnerable to RSLR driven by climate change and local subsidence. A RSLR of just +0.3 m is projected to generate almost 100 closures of the Mo.S.E. (Experimental Electromechanical Module) system per year, often involving multiple tidal cycles and significantly disrupting vessel traffic and lagoon flushing.
- **Thresholds:** Safeguard thresholds are set at 1.10 m for Venice (Punta della Salute) and Burano, and 1.30 m for Chioggia.
- **Scenario Comparison (IPCC):** The study evaluates RSLR up to +0.5 m, noting that +0.5 m slightly exceeds the projected RSLR for the northern Adriatic Sea by 2100 under the IPCC RCP 2.6 scenario.
- **Annual Events Over Threshold:** Without protection, the number of annual flood events exceeding the 1.10 m threshold increases sharply with RSLR. At +0.5 m RSLR, nearly 50% of flood events would still exceed safeguard levels even with the partial closure of the Lido inlet (PCL).

### Mo.S.E. Barrier Effectiveness and Management
- **Full vs. Partial Closure:** Frequent full closure of all three inlets (Lido, Malamocco, Chioggia) negatively impacts water quality (reduced flushing), the fishing industry, and port activities. The study proposes a 'Partial Closure of the Lagoon' (PCL) by closing only the Lido inlet (the closest to Venice).
- **PCL Effectiveness:** Closing only the Lido inlet provides a reduction in sea level (SL) peaks through a linear relationship with the tidal range:
  - **Venice (Punta della Salute):** ~20% reduction (e.g., 0.12 m reduction for a 0.6 m tidal range).
  - **Burano:** ~27% reduction (0.16 m for a 0.6 m tidal range).
  - **Chioggia:** ~4% reduction (minimal benefit of 0.02 m).
- **Adaptation Capacity:** PCL can effectively protect urban settlements from approximately two-thirds of flooding events for RSLR scenarios up to +0.4 m. For RSLR of +0.5 m, PCL fails to prevent flooding in roughly 50% of cases, necessitating full Mo.S.E. closures.
- **Operational Constraints:** 
  - **Wind Set-up:** North-easterly (Bora) winds generally assist in reducing SL peaks during PCL. However, south-easterly (Sirocco) winds can be detrimental, potentially increasing SL peaks by up to 0.1 m during a PCL, which would require full system closure.
  - **Seiche Waves:** Events involving multiple tidal cycles (longitudinal seiche in the Adriatic) can reduce PCL effectiveness.
  - **Infiltration:** Intra-gate infiltration through barriers accounts for 5%–10% of the calculated SL peak reduction.

### Hydrodynamic Impacts
- **Sub-basin Alteration:** PCL depletes the Lido sub-basin, causing an expansion of the Malamocco sub-basin and shifting the Chioggia sub-basin. It increases incoming flow rates through the Malamocco inlet by ~22% and Chioggia inlet by ~8% compared to an unregulated lagoon.

sources.title: The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice
sources.content:
Projected sea level rise (SLR) impacts on Venice, flood frequency, and MOSE barrier effectiveness according to the 2023 TU Delft research by Robin Oomens:

### Sea Level Rise Projections for Venice
*   **Historical Trend:** Relative SLR (RSL) is approximately 2.38 mm/yr (comprising 1.38 mm/yr mean SLR and 1 mm/yr land subsidence).
*   **IPCC/NASA Projections (Median Values by 2100):**
    *   **RCP 2.6 (Low emission):** 42 cm SLR by 2100.
    *   **RCP 4.5 (Moderate):** 52 cm SLR by 2100.
    *   **RCP 7.0 (High):** 59 cm SLR by 2100.
*   **2050 Projections:** SLR is projected to reach approximately 15 cm (RCP 2.6) to 17 cm (RCP 7.0).

### Flood Frequency Projections (MOSE Barrier Closures)
The MOSE barrier is triggered when water levels are forecast to exceed **110 cm + ZMPS** (Zero Mareografico Punta della Salute).
*   **Current Baseline (2020):** Approximately 5.8 to 6 closures per year.
*   **2050 Projections:**
    *   **RCP 2.6:** 24 closures per year.
    *   **RCP 4.5:** 26 closures per year.
    *   **RCP 7.0:** 28 closures per year.
*   **2100 Projections:**
    *   **RCP 2.6:** 209 closures per year.
    *   **RCP 4.5:** 308 closures per year.
    *   **RCP 7.0:** 353 closures per year.
*   **Critical Threshold:** If SLR reaches **60 cm**, the barrier will likely need to be closed year-round, essentially becoming a permanent flood wall rather than a storm surge barrier.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Operational Limits
*   **Functionality Shift:** The barrier was not designed for near-permanent closure. Frequent closures (exceeding ~50 cm SLR) jeopardize its function as a mobile storm surge barrier, threatening the lagoon's ecological balance and blocking essential ship traffic.
*   **Duration:** Average closure duration is currently 4–5 hours but is expected to increase as storm surges lengthen due to climate change.
*   **Operational Costs:** Each closure costs approximately **€972,700**, which includes:
    *   Standard operating/personnel costs: €300,000.
    *   Port activity/economic damage: €70,000.
    *   Flood damage to buildings (at 110 cm): €602,700.
*   **Maintenance:** Annual maintenance is estimated at **€80 million**. High frequency of use will further increase maintenance loads and reduce the time available for repairs.

### Adaptation Strategy: Lifting Venice
The study evaluated raising the entire city by **30 cm** through seawater injection into deep soil layers.
*   **Effectiveness:** In an RCP 4.5 scenario for 2100, lifting the city reduces annual closures from **308 to 44**. 
*   **Economic Impact:** Across all SLR scenarios, the "adapt" strategy (lifting the city) resulted in smaller "expected regrets" (monetary losses) compared to relying solely on the MOSE barrier. The cost for this adaptation is estimated between **€200–300 million**.

sources.title: The Impact of MOSE Flood Barriers on Microphytobenthic Community of the Venice Lagoon
sources.content:
The provided research article discusses the impact of the MOSE (Experimental Electromechanical Module) flood barriers on the Venice Lagoon, specifically referencing sea level rise (SLR) projections and operational frequency:

### Sea Level Rise Projections for Venice
- **Projected Rise:** Sea level is predicted to rise between **+0.6 m and +1.2 m** by the end of the 21st century (citing IPCC 2021/Ref 1).
- **Vulnerability:** Coastal and lagoonal areas like Venice are among the most vulnerable to these climate-driven derangements.

### MOSE Barrier Operations and Flood Frequency
- **Activation Threshold:** The MOSE system consists of mobile gates at the three lagoon inlets (Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia). The gates are raised to isolate the lagoon when flood events with a tide predicted above **110 cm** occur.
- **Current Frequency:** Since the MOSE start-up, very high tide conditions (requiring closure) have occurred approximately **4 times per year**. Typical closures last from a few hours up to 24 hours.
- **Future Projections:** In the near future, it is predicted that the barriers will need to be closed **more than 200 times per year**, totaling approximately **1,000 hours of closure annually** (citing Umgiesser/Toso et al.).

### MOSE Effectiveness and Scenarios
- **Limits of Effectiveness:** Recent studies cited in the article indicate that the MOSE system will only be effective for **moderate sea level rises**. 
- **Ecological Compromise:** As sea levels rise, the increasingly frequent and prolonged closures required to protect the city will have severe ecological consequences. These include:
  - **Oxygen Depletion:** Repeated closures are predicted to rapidly deplete dissolved oxygen levels in the lagoon, particularly when water temperatures exceed 20 °C.
  - **Hydrodynamic Alteration:** Closures limit the exchange of oxygen and nutrients with the open sea and lengthen the water renewal period.
  - **Sediment and Community Shifts:** Reduced hydrodynamics favor the sinking of particulate matter and planktonic cells (like *Thalassiosira* diatoms) to the sediment, altering the microphytobenthic (MPB) community structure and potentially causing persistent hypoxic/anoxic events during warm seasons.

### Environmental Context of the Venice Lagoon
- **Physical Scale:** The lagoon covers 550 km² with an average depth of 1.5 m. Residence times for water range from 24 hours near inlets to 30 days in internal areas.
- **Anthropogenic Stress:** Since 2004, the morphology of the inlets has been heavily modified for MOSE construction, altering fine sediment exchange between the basin and the Adriatic Sea.

sources.title: IPCC AR6 WG2 Figure Box 13.1.1 | Venice Sea Level Rise and Coastal Flooding
sources.content:
Figure Box 13.1.1 from the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (Working Group II, Chapter 13) details the projected impacts of sea level rise (SLR) on Venice, focusing on flood frequency and critical management thresholds.

### Historical Context and Trends
- **Relative Sea Level (RSL) and Mean Sea Level**: The figure tracks the historical evolution of RSL in Venice alongside the decadal frequency of floods exceeding the "safeguard level" in the city center (source: Frederikse et al.).
- **Safeguard Level**: This refers to the tidal threshold (traditionally 110 cm) above which the city center experiences significant flooding. The frequency of events exceeding this level has historically increased.

### Projected Sea Level Rise (SLR)
- **Regional Projections**: Projected relative SLR at the Venetian coast is based on Fox-Kemper et al. (2021). These projections incorporate local subsidence and regional oceanographic factors.
- **Confidence Ranges**: Data is provided for "very likely" (5–95th percentile) and "likely" (17–83rd percentile) ranges.
- **SSP Scenarios**: Projections are evaluated across various Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs).
- **High-Impact Storyline (SSP5-8.5)**: Under the high-emission SSP5-8.5 scenario, the upper limit of the medium confidence range represents a "low-likelihood, high-impact storyline." This includes low-confidence processes such as ice sheet instability, which could lead to significantly higher RSL than the median projections.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Critical Thresholds
- **Critical Threshold Timing**: The figure (part c) estimates when Venice will reach "critical relative sea level thresholds" depending on the scenario and confidence level (citing Lionello 2012; Umgiesser 2020; Lionello et al. 2021a).
- **MOSE Context**: While the MOSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) system—comprising barriers at the Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia inlets—is designed to protect Venice from floods, these "critical thresholds" represent points where the system's operational effectiveness may be compromised. 
- **Operational Limits**: As RSL rises, the frequency of MOSE closures must increase to maintain the safeguard level. Research cited (Umgiesser 2020) suggests that beyond certain SLR thresholds (often identified as 30 cm to 60 cm), the barriers would need to be closed so frequently that it would disrupt lagoon ecology (becoming a "stagnant lake") and maritime commerce.

### Geographical Components
- The Venetian Lagoon is connected to the Adriatic Sea via three inlets: Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia. The figure includes a Landsat view showing these locations, which are the sites of the MOSE barrier infrastructure.

sources.title: Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - Abstract
sources.content:
This study (Trippanera et al., 2025) presents projected relative sea level rise (RSLR) and flooding impacts for the Venice Lagoon for the years 2050, 2100, and 2150, based on IPCC AR6 scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5).

### Subsidence and Vertical Land Movement (VLM)
- Subsidence rates in the Lagoon are inhomogeneous.
- While Venice's ancient city center is considered relatively stable, the three inlets where the MOSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers are located are experiencing subsidence rates of up to 2.9 mm/yr (based on GNSS and InSAR data from 1996-2023).

### Flooding Projections for 2150
- **Land Area at Risk**: Under the SSP1-2.6 scenario (low emissions), approximately 112 km² of land is projected to be at risk. Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario (high emissions), this increases to 159 km².
- **Extreme Water Levels**: Factoring in historical extreme events (astronomical tides, seiches, and atmospheric forcing) combined with RSLR, water levels could temporarily reach up to 3.47 m by 2150.
- **Flooding Extent**: An extreme scenario of 3.47 m would result in up to 65% of the total lagoon land being flooded. In low-lying areas like Chioggia, up to 90% of the land would be submerged.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness
The study concludes that these projected RSLR values and extreme water levels pose significant challenges to the durability and effectiveness of the MOSE barrier system. While MOSE currently protects against high tides, the accelerating sea level rise and localized subsidence at the barrier inlets raise questions about its long-term viability through 2150.

The same IPCC box notes that critical relative sea-level thresholds are reached at different times depending on scenario, and that the upper end of the SSP5-8.5 medium-confidence range reflects a low-likelihood, high-impact storyline involving low-confidence ice-sheet-instability processes. sources.title: How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint - ScienceDirect
sources.content: The study evaluates Mo.S.E. barrier management under climate change and different relative sea-level rise scenarios. It finds that the enhanced wind setup constraint will lead the Mo.S.E. system to fail in controlling all flood events starting from a relative sea level rise of +0.40 m. It also states that wind setup will increasingly often produce long and repeated closures, and that the system only protects all urban settlements when closure strategy accounts for these dynamics.

sources.title: Return periods of extreme sea levels: From magnitude to frequency, duration and seasonality. Implications in a regulated coastal lagoon - ScienceDirect
sources.content: Using 96 years of sea-level records, the study models the frequency and duration of extreme sea levels in the Venice lagoon while accounting for seasonality and mean sea-level rise. It projects the yearly occurrence of extreme sea levels to increase almost up to 10-fold compared to current conditions with mean sea level of +80 cm, reaching values higher than 20 clusters per year. It also says that with mean sea level of +80 cm, durations can increase up to 200 h, implying longer lagoon disconnections from the sea. The paper states that with +50 cm sea level, the MOSE barriers will have to be activated almost every day because tide alone exceeds the 1.10 m flooding threshold.

sources.title: Venice as a paradigm of coastal flooding under multiple compound drivers | Scientific Reports
sources.content: The paper reports that if nonlinear tide–non-tidal residual interactions are ignored, return sea levels are overestimated and extreme sea levels could occur with higher frequency. It gives historical nuisance-flood counts for Venice: 8 in 1890–1919, 44 in 1940–1969, and 172 in 1990–2019. It concludes flood risk increased during the last century and will worsen in the future due to relative sea-level rise. It specifically states that with a sea-level rise of 50 cm, the MOSE barriers will need to be activated almost every day because tide alone would exceed the 1.10 m flood threshold.

sources.title: Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy | npj Climate and Atmospheric Science
sources.content: The study compares factual-present conditions where MoSE can be activated with counterfactual conditions where it is not operative. It says the authors evaluate MoSE effectiveness by comparing sea level and estimated damages for analogues of extreme events. It reports that MoSE has already functioned as an effective mitigation strategy for the 1966 analogue set, with damages reduced from 0.45 MEUR to 0.25 MEUR in one analysis. It also states that the 1966 event had 11 analogues in the factual-present period. The daily operational cost of MoSE is estimated at 0.025 MEUR in the analysis.

sources.title: Venice’s flood barriers are working overtime. How will they change the lagoon?
sources.content: This Nature article reports that climate change and rising sea levels are making barrier closures more frequent and says some projections indicate that by the end of the century, in a no-mitigation climate scenario, MOSE could be activated and the lagoon closed for approximately 260 days per year. It notes the barriers have been highly effective in safeguarding Venice from acqua alta, but increased closure frequency may affect the lagoon ecosystem.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna 2022–2026?
synthesis:
Based on the provided ICCAT recommendations and management plans, the Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for the period 2022–2026 are as follows:

*   **2022:** The TAC was set at **36,000 tonnes (t)**, including dead discards, under Recommendation 21-08 [ICCAT Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf).
*   **2023–2025:** The annual TAC was increased to **40,570 t** for each of these three years following the adoption of a new Management Procedure (MP) established in Recommendation 22-08 [ICCAT Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT Management Quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2022–2026)](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp).
*   **2026:** The annual TAC for the 2026–2028 management cycle is set at **48,403 t** per year [ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf) [ICCAT Meeting Sets New Tuna TACs And Shark Conservation ...](https://thefishingdaily.com/latest-news/iccat-meeting-sets-new-tuna-tacs-and-shark-conservation-measures/). This represents a 17% increase from the previous cycle [EU Secures 17% Increase of Bluefin Tuna Fishing Quota at ICCAT](https://thefishingdaily.com/latest-news/eu-secures-17-increase-of-bluefin-tuna-fishing-quota-at-iccat).

### **Specific Allocations (Top CPCs)**
The European Union holds the largest portion of these quotas, with its allocation increasing from **19,460 t** in 2022 to **21,503 t** in 2023–2025, and finally to **25,164.62 t** for 2026 [Total Allowable Catch (TAC) and national quotas of Atlantic bluefin tuna](https://oprt.or.jp/pdf/TAC_Atlantic_bluefin_tuna_eng.pdf) [ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf). Other major allocations for the 2026–2028 period include:
- **Morocco:** 4,379.47 t
- **Japan:** 3,559.41 t
- **Tunisia:** 3,508.85 t
- **T rkiye:** 3,094.45 t
- **Libya:** 2,950.03 t
- **Algeria:** 2,443.64 t [ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf).

### **Data Contradictions**
There is a discrepancy regarding the 2026 TAC in one source. While the primary management plan (Rec. 2025-04) and recent reports confirm a TAC of 48,403 t [ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf), another document (Rec. 25-05) suggests that the 2026 TAC was maintained at 40,570 t based on 2024 Commission meeting results [ICCAT Management Plans and TAC Quotas for Bluefin Tuna (Rec. 25-05 and associated)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-05-e.pdf). However, multiple external sources and the more detailed recommendation 2025-04 align on the 48,403 t figure representing the updated management period [EU Secures 17% Increase of Bluefin Tuna Fishing Quota at ICCAT](https://thefishingdaily.com/latest-news/eu-secures-17-increase-of-bluefin-tuna-fishing-quota-at-iccat).

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation 22-08: Multi-annual Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna
sources.content:
According to ICCAT Recommendation 22-08 (Amending Recommendation 21-08), the following Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas and allocations have been set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna:

### TAC for 2023–2025
The Total Allowable Catch (TAC), inclusive of dead discards, is set at **40,570 tonnes (t)** annually for the years 2023, 2024, and 2025. This TAC was established based on the Management Procedure (MP) adopted in Recommendation 22-09.

### Annual Quota Allocation for 2023–2025 (in tonnes):
- **European Union:** 21,503 t
- **Morocco:** 3,700 t
- **Japan:** 3,114 t
- **Tunisia:** 3,000 t
- **Türkiye:** 2,600 t
- **Libya:** 2,548 t
- **Algeria:** 2,023 t
- **Egypt:** 513 t
- **Norway:** 368 t
- **Albania:** 264 t
- **Iceland:** 224 t
- **Korea:** 221 t
- **Syria:** 129 t
- **China:** 112 t
- **Chinese Taipei:** 101 t
- **United Kingdom:** 63 t
- **Namibia:** 50 t
- **Unallocated Reserves:** 37 t
- **TOTAL:** 40,570 t

### Specific Conditions and Provisions:
- **Research Catches:** Mauritania and Senegal may catch up to 5 t each per year for research purposes, to be deducted from the unallocated reserve.
- **Quota Transfers:** Chinese Taipei may transfer up to 50 t of its quota to Korea annually (2023–2025). CPCs may also transfer portions of their quota to other CPCs subject to prior notification to the ICCAT Secretariat.
- **Carry-over:** Automatic carry-over of unused quota is not authorized. CPCs may request to transfer a maximum of 5% of their annual quota to the following year, subject to Commission endorsement.
- **2022 TAC:** While Recommendation 22-08 focuses on 2023–2025, it amends Recommendation 21-08, which set the **2022 TAC at 36,000 t**.
- **2026 TAC:** The TAC for 2026 and subsequent years will be decided at the **2025 Commission Annual Meeting** in accordance with the Management Procedure (MP).

### Management Measures:
- **Minimum Size:** Generally 30 kg or 115 cm fork length. Derogations allow for 8 kg or 75 cm fork length for specific fisheries (e.g., baitboats/trolling in the Eastern Atlantic, small-scale coastal fleets in the Mediterranean, and Adriatic farming).
- **Purse Seine Season:** Permitted from 26 May to 1 July (with specific derogations for the Eastern Mediterranean, Adriatic Sea, Norwegian/Icelandic zones, and Morocco).
- **Longline Season:** Large-scale pelagic longliners (>24m) are permitted from 1 January to 31 May (with area-specific exceptions).

sources.title: ICCAT Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2025-04)
sources.content:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) has set the following Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (Thunnus thynnus) for the period 2022–2026:

### **TAC Quotas by Year**
*   **2022:** The TAC was set at **36,000 t** under Recommendation 19-04 (the Recovery Plan).
*   **2023:** The TAC was set at **40,570 t** per year under Recommendation 22-08.
*   **2024:** The TAC was set at **40,570 t** per year under Recommendation 22-08.
*   **2025:** The TAC was set at **40,570 t** per year under Recommendation 22-08.
*   **2026:** The TAC is set at **48,403 t** per year (for the 2026–2028 period) in accordance with the Management Procedure (MP) adopted in 2022, as established in the 2025-04 recommendation replacing Rec. 24-05.

### **Specific CPC Quota Allocations for 2026–2028 (Total 48,403 t/year):**
*   **European Union:** 25,164.62 t
*   **Morocco:** 4,379.47 t
*   **Japan:** 3,559.41 t
*   **Tunisia:** 3,508.85 t
*   **Türkiye:** 3,094.45 t
*   **Libya:** 2,950.03 t
*   **Algeria:** 2,443.64 t (includes a 17 t transfer from unallocated reserves)
*   **Egypt:** 549 t
*   **Norway:** 461.38 t
*   **Albania:** 457.37 t
*   **Korea:** 368.93 t
*   **China:** 286.15 t
*   **Iceland:** 253.14 t
*   **Syria:** 238 t
*   **United Kingdom:** 230.56 t
*   **Chinese Taipei:** 101 t (may transfer up to 50 t to Korea)
*   **Mauritania, Namibia, Panama:** 55 t each
*   **Research Reserve:** 20 t
*   **Unallocated Reserves:** 117 t (100 t for bycatch in longline fisheries and 17 t for Algeria)

### **Key Management Measures Associated with Quotas:**
*   **Management Plan implementation:** CPCS must implement a management plan starting in 2023 based on the Management Procedure (MP) defined in Rec. 23-07.
*   **Carry-over:** Automatic carry-over of unused quota is prohibited. CPCs may request to transfer a maximum of 20% of their annual quota to the following year, subject to Commission endorsement.
*   **Bycatch:** All CPCs must allocate a specific quota for bycatch, which must not exceed 20% of the total catches on board at the end of a trip. Dead discards are counted against the quota.
*   **Minimum Size:** Generally 30 kg or 115 cm fork length, with derogations allowing 8 kg or 75 cm for specific small-scale coastal fleets and baitboats.
*   **Purse Seine Season:** Permitted from 19 May to 1 July annually, with specific derogations for the Adriatic Sea (26 May to 15 July) and the Norwegian/Icelandic zones (25 June to 15 November).

sources.title: ICCAT Management Quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (2022–2026)
sources.content:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) has established specific Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for the period 2022–2026 based on stock assessments and the recently adopted Management Procedure (MP).

### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022–2025)
- **2022:** The TAC was set at **36,000 t** (under Recommendation 21-08, which amended previous plans). This figure included dead discards.
- **2023–2025:** Following the adoption of the Management Procedure (MP) in 2022 (Rec. 22-09), the TAC was increased to **40,570 t** per year for each of the three years (Recommendation 22-08). This TAC is inclusive of dead discards.
- **2026:** The TAC for 2026 and the subsequent management cycle (2026–2028) is scheduled to be decided at the **2025 Commission Annual Meeting** (typically held in November) based on the results of the MP and scientific advice from the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS).

### Annual Quota Allocation (2023–2025)
The annual TAC of 40,570 t for the period 2023–2025 is allocated among Contracting Parties (CPCs) as follows (top allocations):
- **European Union:** 21,503 t
- **Morocco:** 3,700 t
- **Japan:** 3,114 t
- **Tunisia:** 3,000 t
- **Türkiye:** 2,600 t
- **Libya:** 2,548 t
- **Algeria:** 2,023 t
- **Egypt:** 513 t
- **Norway:** 368 t
- **Albania:** 264 t
- **Iceland:** 224 t
- **Korea:** 221 t
- **Syria:** 129 t
- **China:** 112 t
- **Chinese Taipei:** 101 t
- **United Kingdom:** 63 t
- **Namibia:** 50 t
- **Unallocated Reserves:** 37 t

### Management Framework and MP Details
- **Management Procedure (Rec. 22-09):** The 'BR Management Procedure' was selected in 2022. It is an empirical approach using standardized abundance indices (weighted average of five indices for the East area) to calculate TACs every three years.
- **Stability Clause:** Any change in TAC between consecutive management periods should generally not exceed a 20% increase or a 35% decrease. However, for the first application cycle (2023–2025), a phase-in period limited any decrease to no more than 10%.
- **Exceptional Circumstances (Rec. 23-07):** An Exceptional Circumstances Protocol (ECP) was adopted in 2023 to allow the Commission to suspend or modify the MP if stock or fishery dynamics fall outside the ranges tested during Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE). As of 2024, no exceptional circumstances were declared, though the SCRS reviewed potential triggers in 2025.

sources.title: ICCAT Management Plans and TAC Quotas for Bluefin Tuna (Rec. 25-05 and associated)
sources.content:
Based on ICCAT Recommendations and the Management Procedure (MP) adopted in 2022 (Rec. 22-09), the following Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas have been set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for the period 2022–2026:

- **2022:** 36,000 t (as established by Recommendation 21-08).
- **2023:** 40,570 t (established via the first application of the Management Procedure under Recommendation 22-08).
- **2024:** 40,570 t (maintained under the multi-year management plan in Rec. 22-08, as amended by Rec. 23-06).
- **2025:** 40,570 t (maintained under Rec. 22-08 / Rec. 23-06).
- **2026:** 40,570 t (The Management Procedure results from the 2024 Commission meeting, codified in Recommendation 25-04, set the annual TAC for 2026–2028 at 40,570 t, assuming no exceptional circumstances are confirmed).

### Key Context from Scraped Document (Rec. 25-05):
While the provided document (Rec. 25-05) primarily establishes the management plan for **Western Atlantic** bluefin tuna (setting a TAC of 3,081.6 t for 2026–2028), it contains several specific data points relevant to the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean stock:
- **Shared Management Procedure:** The Commission adopted a Management Procedure (MP) in 2022 (Rec. 22-09) to establish TACs for **both** the Eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean and Western Atlantic management areas starting in 2023.
- **Eastern TAC Derivations:** Specific allocations for bycatch in longline fisheries near the management boundary are derived directly from the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean TAC. These include:
  - **United States:** 62.5 t
  - **Canada:** 37.5 t
- **Exceptional Circumstances:** In 2025, the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) assessed the occurrence of exceptional circumstances but was unable to reach a consensus. Per the protocol (Rec. 23-07), when exceptional circumstances are not confirmed, the TAC produced by the MP is used.
- **Effort Prohibitions:** CPCs (Contracting Parties) are prohibited from transferring fishing effort between the Western Atlantic and the Eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean to avoid increasing fishing mortality in either area.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado, main commodity drivers, and specific IBAMA enforcement actions in the last two years?
synthesis:
Deforestation in the Brazilian Cerrado has surpassed the Amazon in recent years, with the biome losing 1.1 million hectares in 2023, more than double the rate of the Amazon at that time [Deforestation in Brazil’s Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains](https://zerocarbon-analytics.org/insights/briefings/deforestation-in-brazils-cerrado-reduces-soy-production-and-threatens-supply-chains/). In 2024, the Cerrado lost 652,197 hectares of native vegetation, remaining Brazil's most deforested biome [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/). However, official data for the 2024-2025 period indicates an 11.49% decrease in deforestation compared to the previous year [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by ...](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado). The Matopiba region (Maranho, Tocantins, Piauv, and Bahia) remains the primary hotspot, accounting for 75% of all Cerrado clearing in 2024 [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/).

The main commodity drivers are cattle ranching and soybean production. Cattle ranching is identified as the largest direct driver of land conversion [Deforestation in Brazil’s Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains](https://zerocarbon-analytics.org/insights/briefings/deforestation-in-brazils-cerrado-reduces-soy-production-and-threatens-supply-chains/). Soy is the second largest driver, with major producers such as Mizote Group and Franciosi Agro linked to supply chains involving global traders like Cargill and Bunge [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/). China is the primary export destination, purchasing approximately 76% to 79.9% of Brazil's soy [Deforestation in Brazil’s Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains](https://zerocarbon-analytics.org/insights/briefings/deforestation-in-brazils-cerrado-reduces-soy-production-and-threatens-supply-chains/).

IBAMA enforcement actions in 2023 and 2024 have intensified through several strategic measures:
- **Increased Field Operations:** In 2023, infraction notices for crimes against flora in the Cerrado increased by 45% compared to the 2019-2022 average. During the same period, embargoes rose by 43%, seizures by 25%, and the destruction of illegal equipment by 124% [‘Only 2% of rural landowners are non-compliant with environmental legislation’ – Interview with the President of Ibama, Brazil](https://agribrasilis.com/2024/02/27/ibama-brazil1/).
- **Operation Caixa-Forte:** This operation targets financial institutions, holding them liable if they provide rural credit to properties with illegal deforestation [The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte](https://www.felsberg.com.br/en/the-liability-of-financial-institutions-in-brazil-for-environmental-violations-reflections-from-operation-caixa-forte/).
- **Policy Reversals:** The current administration nullified previous decrees that had granted amnesty for approximately R$ 29 billion ($5.8 billion USD) in environmental fines [Rectifying the damage: environmental fines in the Brazilian Amazon](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/05/rectifying-the-damage-environmental-fines-in-the-brazilian-amazon/).
- **Fine Conversion Program:** IBAMA is implementing a program to convert R$ 30 billion in outstanding fines into environmental recovery projects, offering offenders up to 60% reductions if they participate in restoration [Bureaucracy Hinders Ibama Program to Convert Fines of $5 Billion into Environmental Recovery](https://www1.folha.uol.com.br/internacional/en/scienceandhealth/2024/12/bureaucracy-hinders-ibama-program-to-convert-fines-of-5-billion-into-environmental-recovery.shtml).
- **Remote Inspection:** The agency has expanded the use of satellite technology and database integration (such as CAR) to issue fines and embargoes without field presence [‘Only 2% of rural landowners are non-compliant with environmental legislation’ – Interview with the President of Ibama, Brazil](https://agribrasilis.com/2024/02/27/ibama-brazil1/).

sources.title: Rectifying the damage: environmental fines in the Brazilian Amazon
sources.content:
While the provided article focuses primarily on the Brazilian Amazon, it details significant policy-level IBAMA enforcement actions relevant to the last two years (2023-2025 context):

- **Reversal of Amnesty Decrees:** The current Brazilian administration has reversed a decree from the previous Bolsonaro regime that had pardoned more than 180,000 environmental fine cases. This action includes a legal brief that nullified a decree granting amnesty for 185,000 administrative rulings, which represented approximately R$ 29 billion (roughly $5.8 billion USD) in fines for illegal land clearing and logging.
- **IBAMA Fine Collection Challenges:** Historically, IBAMA's fine collection rate has been extremely low. Between 2010 and 2016, approximately 8,000 fines totaling over R$ 4 billion were levied annually, but payments never exceeded 4%. By 2017, arrears surpassed R$ 23 billion. Large corporations like Petrobras reportedly have outstanding fines in the range of R$ 1.3 billion.
- **Enforcement Trends:** Under the previous administration (2019-2022), IBAMA's capacity was weakened via budget cuts and management changes, leading to a 20% drop in fines in 2020 (9,516 fines) compared to 2019 (11,914 fines). The current administration is working to reinstate these oversight mechanisms.
- **Drivers of Deforestation:** The article identifies cattle confinement as a major factor affecting ecosystems in the Brazilian Amazon, though it does not provide specific statistics for the Cerrado biome.

[Note: This specific source does not contain the current Cerrado deforestation rate or commodity drivers specific to the Cerrado; these details may be found in the suggested 'Brasil' or 'Deforestation' links.]

sources.content:

sources.title: The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte
sources.content:
The article discusses the legal implications for financial institutions regarding environmental violations in Brazil, specifically reflecting on IBAMA's "Operation Caixa-Forte" (Operation Strongbox).

Key Enforcement Actions and Mechanisms:
- Operation Caixa-Forte: This IBAMA operation targets the liability of financial institutions that provide rural credit to properties with illegal deforestation. It focuses on the intersection of environmental enforcement and the financial system.
- Information Integration: IBAMA utilizes various databases for inspections, including rural property records (CAR), vegetation suppression authorizations, and data on rural credit operations.
- Rural Credit Restrictions: According to the article, rural credit will not be granted for businesses located on rural properties where there is a valid embargo by a competent federal or state environmental authority. This restriction applies specifically when the embargo results from the economic use of illegally deforested areas on the property.
- Registration Requirements: For the credit restriction to take effect, the property must be registered in the list of embargoes of the Environmental Infractions and Embargoes Register of IBAMA.
- Legal Framework: The enforcement aligns with Articles 7 and 8 of Complementary Law No. 140/2011, which outlines environmental competencies.

Context for the Cerrado (Savanna):
The article is tagged with "#savanna," indicating its relevance to enforcement actions in the Cerrado biome, where rural credit is a primary lever for commodity production. The focus on Operation Caixa-Forte highlights a shift toward holding financial backers accountable for illegal land clearing in these regions.

sources.title: Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rate in the Brazilian Cerrado
- **2024 Figures**: The Cerrado lost **652,197 hectares (1.61 million acres)** of native vegetation in 2024. For the second consecutive year, it was the most deforested biome in Brazil, surpassing the Amazon.
- **Long-term Trends**: Agricultural activity in the Cerrado expanded by 74% between 1985 and 2024. Currently, just over half of the biome is covered in native vegetation, and one in every four municipalities has less than 20% native cover remaining.
- **Hotspots**: The **Matopiba** region (comprising parts of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia) accounted for **75% of all Cerrado deforestation** in 2024.

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Soy Production**: Soy is the primary driver of land conversion. Investigations by Earthsight linked soy produced on deforested land to supply chains for McDonald’s and major European supermarkets.
- **Key Companies**: Two major producers, **Mizote Group** and **Franciosi Agro**, were found to have cleared 986 hectares (2,436 acres) since May 2024. Between January 2021 and April 2024, these producers cleared 23,164 hectares (57,239 acres) in western Bahia.
- **Supply Chain Links**: These producers sell soy to global commodity traders **Cargill and Bunge**, who facilitate approximately 95% of soy exports from western Bahia to the European Union.
- **Legal Loopholes**: While the EU Antideforestation Regulation (EUDR) aims to ban products from deforested land, it currently uses a FAO definition of 'forest' that excludes approximately 74% of the Cerrado (savannas and grasslands).

### Enforcement and Governance Actions
- **State-Level Permits**: Earthsight found that 60% of the area cleared by Mizote and Franciosi lacked identifiable deforestation permits. However, more recent clearances were technically permitted by **Inema** (the Bahia state environmental agency), though they remain non-compliant with EUDR standards.
- **Certification Suspensions**: The Roundtable on Responsible Soy (RTRS) suspended the certificates of **Franciosi Agro** and **Horita Group** in 2024 following investigations into land grabbing and deforestation allegations.
- **Policy Setbacks**: The **Soy Moratorium** (a voluntary agreement to curb deforestation in the Amazon) is slated for suspension in 2026, which experts fear will further weaken conservation efforts across Brazilian biomes.
- **IBAMA Note**: The provided article does not detail specific IBAMA (federal) enforcement actions, focusing instead on state-level authorizations (Inema) and private sector certifications (RTRS). [Further research into IBAMA's public embargo database would be required for specific federal fine/embargo data].

sources.title: Deforestation in Brazil’s Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains
sources.content:
### Deforestation Rates and Context
- **2023 Rate:** Deforestation in the Brazilian Cerrado reached 1.1 million hectares in 2023, more than double the rate of the Amazon in the same year. 
- **Current Trend:** Recent data indicates a 20.8% decrease in deforestation alerts, covering 5,555 km² compared to 7,014 km² in the previous period (based on August 2025 reporting figures).
- **Historical Extent:** At least 46% of the Cerrado’s native vegetation has been cleared for pasture or crops.

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Primary Drivers:** Cattle ranching (pasture expansion) is the largest direct driver of deforestation and conversion in the Cerrado.
- **Secondary Driver:** Soy production is the second-largest direct driver. Brazil is the world’s largest producer and exporter of soy, which accounted for 14.5% of total exports between January and September 2025.
- **Matopiba Region:** This region (comprising Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia) is identified as the active agricultural frontier where recent soy expansion and associated clearing are concentrated.

### Economic and Climate Impacts
- **Rainfall Reduction:** Deforestation in the Cerrado reduces regional rainfall and disrupts 'forest-rainfall teleconnections.' This leads to shorter growing seasons for rain-fed crops.
- **Yield Losses:** Modeling shows that if deforestation-induced rainfall disruption hadn't occurred since the 1980s, soybean yields would have been 6.6% higher annually between 2011 and 2020. 
- **Revenue Risk:** Weak deforestation policies could lead to an estimated USD 186 billion loss in soy and beef revenues by 2050. Conversely, avoiding just 10% of clearing between 2013-2023 could have generated an additional USD 938.8 million in soy value.

### Supply Chain and Enforcement Context
- **Major Importers:** China is the largest partner, purchasing approximately 76% to 79.9% of Brazil's total soy exports in 2024-2025. The EU is the second-largest destination at approximately 7%.
- **EU Regulation (EUDR):** The EU Deforestation Regulation, signed in June 2023 and expected to be fully implemented by December 2026, prohibits imports from land deforested since December 31, 2020. 
- **Enforcement and IBAMA:** The document notes that Brazil’s deforestation protection policies and the thoroughness of their enforcement (implied IBAMA/governmental action) directly impact the risk exposure of global supply chains. However, specific named IBAMA enforcement operations or granular statistics for the last two years are not detailed in this briefing; it focuses instead on the economic consequences of policy failures and the potential for collective action through mechanisms like the Soy Moratorium.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado, main commodity drivers, and specific IBAMA enforcement actions in the last two years?
synthesis:
The most recent official annual figure I found for the Brazilian Cerrado is **8,174 km²** of deforestation for the period **August 2023 to July 2024**, which the Brazilian government said was a **25.8% decline** from the previous period and the **lowest level since 2019**. INPE/MMA later reported that in **2024** the Cerrado’s annual deforestation rate fell by **25.7%** versus 2023, which is consistent with that PRODES release. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/planaltoexciting/es/ultimas-noticias/2024/11/el-gobierno-federal-anuncia-una-reduccion-en-la-deforestacion-en-la-amazonia-y-el-cerrado-y-firma-un-pacto-para-la-prevencion?utm_source=openai))

On the **main commodity drivers**, the strongest official material I found points to **agricultural expansion** in the Cerrado, especially in **Matopiba** (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia). MapBiomas reported that the Cerrado and Amazon together accounted for the vast majority of Brazil’s deforestation alerts and area in 2022, and that soy expansion in the Cerrado has been especially concentrated in Matopiba. MapBiomas also states that the Cerrado has become the leading cause of deforestation in Brazil, and official MMA material describes the biome’s role in production of **soybeans, corn, cotton, and livestock**. ([brasil.mapbiomas.org](https://brasil.mapbiomas.org/en/2023/06/12/desmatamento-nos-biomas-do-brasil-cresceu-223-em-2022/?utm_source=openai))

A 2025 Ibama statement also gives a very direct clue about the current enforcement focus on commodity-linked drivers: in the Cerrado, Ibama said it launched **Operação Caixa-Forte** in **March 2025** to punish financial institutions that granted rural credit to **embargoed farms** linked to illegal deforestation, and the agency explicitly noted that **soy** is the crop with the greatest impact in Matopiba. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente?utm_source=openai))

For **IBAMA enforcement actions in the last two years**, I found several specific actions and aggregate results:
- **Operação Nova Fronteira** (Oct–Nov 2024) in Matopiba: **52 autuações** and about **R$ 206 million** in environmental fines for illegal deforestation, embargo violations, and preventing natural regeneration. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural?utm_source=openai))
- In the broader period **Aug 2023 to Jul 2024**, the federal NDC text says Ibama registered **733 infraction notices**, **449 embargoes**, and **124 seizure notices**, collecting **more than R$ 225 million** in fines in the Cerrado. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/brasil-entrega-a-onu-nova-ndc-alinhada-ao-acordo-de-paris/brazils-ndc.pdf/%40%40download/file?utm_source=openai))
- In **Aug 2024 to May 2025**, Ibama said it applied **R$ 578 million** in fines in the Cerrado, with **737 infraction notices**, **455 embargoes**, **376 seizure notices**, **77 destruction notices**, and **20 civil public actions**. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica?utm_source=openai))
- In **March 2025**, **Operação Caixa-Forte** targeted rural credit supplied to properties embargoed for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado; the published note says the owners were also fined for embargo violations and blocking native vegetation regeneration. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente?utm_source=openai))
- In **June 2025**, **Operação Caryocar** in Maranhão inspected **12 rural properties**, embargoed **more than 1,300 hectares**, and seized about **13.5 tonnes of rice** from openings of new deforestation fronts without valid licensing or vegetation-clearance authorization. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-deflagra-operacao-para-combate-ao-desmatamento-no-cerrado?utm_source=openai))

Bottom line: the Cerrado’s latest official annual deforestation rate is about **8.2 thousand km²**, the main commodity pressure is **soy-led agricultural expansion**—especially across **Matopiba**, alongside broader livestock/agriculture expansion—and Ibama’s enforcement over the last two years has centered on **embargoes, fines, seizures, credit-linked enforcement, and Matopiba operations**. ([gov.br](https://www.gov.br/planaltoexciting/es/ultimas-noticias/2024/11/el-gobierno-federal-anuncia-una-reduccion-en-la-deforestacion-en-la-amazonia-y-el-cerrado-y-firma-un-pacto-para-la-prevencion?utm_source=openai))
